title: Simple Harmonic Motion
learningObjectives: Define simple harmonic motion and its key quantities
Apply the equations x = A cos(ωt), v = -Aω sin(ωt), a = -ω²x
Calculate period for a pendulum and a mass-spring system
introText: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement and directed toward equilibrium. Pendulums and mass-spring systems are classic examples. SHM is elegant because the same equations describe all oscillating systems.

steps.heading: Defining SHM
steps.body:
An object undergoes SHM when its acceleration is:
• Proportional to displacement from equilibrium
• Directed toward equilibrium

**a = −ω²x**

Where:
• x = displacement from equilibrium (m)
• ω = angular frequency (rad/s)
• A = amplitude = maximum displacement (m)

The negative sign means acceleration opposes displacement (restoring).
steps.deepDive: SHM is a solution to the differential equation ẍ + ω²x = 0. The general solution is x = A cos(ωt + φ), where φ is the initial phase. For x = A cos(ωt), the object starts at maximum displacement (x = A) at t = 0.

steps.heading: SHM Equations
steps.body:
Starting from x = A cos(ωt):

• **Displacement:** x = A cos(ωt)
• **Velocity:** v = −Aω sin(ωt) → maximum speed v_max = Aω (at equilibrium)
• **Acceleration:** a = −Aω² cos(ωt) = −ω²x
• **Period:** T = 2π/ω
• **Frequency:** f = 1/T = ω/(2π)

**Speed at displacement x:** v = ω√(A² − x²)
(maximum at x=0, zero at x=±A)

**Period formulae for common systems:**
• Mass on spring: T = 2π√(m/k)
• Simple pendulum (small angle): T = 2π√(L/g)

steps.heading: SHM Simulator
steps.instructionText: Adjust the amplitude and angular frequency. Watch the displacement-time graph and observe how velocity and acceleration change throughout the cycle.
steps.deepDive: The SHM of a pendulum assumes the angle is small (θ < ~15°) so that sin θ ≈ θ. For larger angles the motion is no longer perfectly sinusoidal and the period increases slightly. This is why grandfather clocks have regulated pendulum swing angles.

steps.heading: Energy in SHM
steps.body:
**Kinetic Energy:** KE = ½mv² = ½mω²(A² − x²)
**Potential Energy:** PE = ½mω²x²
**Total Energy:** E = ½mω²A² = constant

At equilibrium (x = 0): all energy is kinetic.
At amplitude (x = ±A): all energy is potential.
Energy continuously interconverts between KE and PE.

Note: E ∝ A² — doubling amplitude quadruples total energy.

steps.questionText: A mass on a spring has amplitude A = 0.1 m and ω = 5 rad/s. What is the maximum speed?
A pendulum of length 1.0 m oscillates on Earth (g = 9.81 m/s²). What is its period?

questions.questionText: In SHM, where is the speed greatest?
options: Halfway between equilibrium and maximum displacement
questions.explanation: Speed is greatest at the equilibrium position (x = 0) where v = Aω, and zero at maximum displacement (x = ±A).

questions.questionText: A mass-spring system has k = 400 N/m and m = 1 kg. What is the period of oscillation?
If the amplitude of an SHM oscillator is doubled, how does the total energy change?
questions.explanation: E = ½mω²A². Since E ∝ A², doubling A quadruples the total energy.
